title: Murine orthotopic pancreatic tumor model generation.
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"ej848","text":"Approximately 50% of pancreatic cancer patients are diagnosed as stage IV with poor prognosis and show treatment resistance to conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy and recently developed immunotherapy. Therefore, there is an urgent need in developing novel treatment options for pancreatic cancer patients and examine them by using proper preclinical models. Murine orthotopic tumor model is generated by seeding murine cancer cell lines or patient-derived cancer cells into the relevant organ of tumor origin. Significance of the orthotopic pancreatic cancer model have been highlited since it can be easily generated compared to GEMM(Genetically engineered mouse models) and represent more clinical relevance than subcutaneous model. Here, we described how to create the murine orthotopic pancreatic cancer model.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cx73x","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"kkzyw","text":" Pancreatic cancer is a type of cancer which starts in the pancreas. There are several types of pancreatic cancer, however more than 90% of pancreatic cancer patients are diagnosed as pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). According to the Global Cancer Observatory (GLOBOCAN) 2020, pancreatic cancer is the 12th most common cancer type and the 7th leading cause of death from cancer1. A 5-year relative survival of pancreatic cancer showed highly poor prognosis of only about 10%. Currently, early diagnosis and timely surgical intervention are the only effective treatment options to significantly improve clinical outcomes of pancreatic cancer patients. However, unfortunately, because of asymptomatic period in early stage of pancreatic cancer, the diagnosis is often delayed. Therefore, approximately 50% of initially diagnosed patients have locally advanced or metastatic disease, and required systemic treatment like chemotherapy2. Furthermore, PDAC has an extremely poor response to chemotherapy, radio-therapy or even recently developed immunotherapy, and therefore urgently needs a new treatment method.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":385,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":938,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5gbcr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dduey","text":" The proper pre-clinical tumor models are required for the development and validation of new therapeutic agents in pancreatic cancer. In standard subcutaneous model, tumor cells are engrafted under the skin on the flank of the animals rather than the organ of tumor origin. Consequently subcutaneous model is not suitable for the the development of new therapeutics because they do not properly reflect the organ-specific tumor microenvironment. GEMM(Genetically engineered mouse models) which spontaneously develop tumors within relevant organs by disease-sepcific mutations and showed anticipating strength for drug response with resistance3. However, the GEMM models has the disadvantages of being expensive and taking a long time to generate tumors,  making it impossible to conduct large-scale experiments. Murine orthotopic tumor model  generated by seeding murine cancer cell lines or patient-derived cancer cells into the relevant organ of tumor origin showed more clinical relevance than subcutaneous model. Moreover, orthotopic model is relatively more convenient for testing a variety of conditions of therapeutic agents than GEMM models because of their ease for production4. Here, we will talk about the construction of a mouse orthotopic pancreatic cancer model.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":642,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":1185,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"polyd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: The procedure is mainly composed of two parts. First, ingredient preparation methods were described. The second part is about how to generate murine orthotopic pancreatic cancer model.

Ketamin/Xylazine mixed solution (KX solution); KX solution was used to anesthetize mice for orthotopic tumor implantation surgery. KX solution is prepared by mixing PBS, Ketamine, and Xylazine with a ratio of 15:4:1.

Ex, for preparing 20mL of KX solution; Mix 15mL of PBS, 4mL of Ketamine Inj. (Ketamine hydrochloride solution made for injection, 50mg/mL as Ketamine and 57.68mg/mL as Ketamine hydrochloride, 유한양행) and 1mL of Xylazine (Xylazine Hydrochloride solution made for injection, 23.32mg/mL as Xylazine Hydrochloride, Elanco). Therefore, 20mL of KX solution contains 200mg of Ketamine (or 230.72mg of Ketamine hydrochloride) and 23.32 mg of Xylazine.
Pancreatic cancer cell solution; Gently resuspend 5x10^5 cells in 20-25 μL of fresh media/Matrigel (1:1) and transfer the solution into 29 gauge insulin syringe.

Caution; Cancer cell condition itself makes a huge difference in tumor growth in vivo, therefore researchers should keep it consistent. We recommended to change the culture media with fresh one 4-8 hrs before detaching the adherent cells from culture plate for the injection. 70% of cancer cell confluency would be a fine condition before the detachment.

Caution; Matrigel is a highly viscous solution and it hardens with irreversible properties when the temperature rises. Hence, it is recommended to keep the cancer cell solution at 4-celsius cold room if possible. If not, keep matrigel in ice and use pipette tips after some refrigeration. Pilot experiment for making fresh media/matrigel (1:1) solution is suggested.

Caution; When transferring the pancreatic cancer cell solution (Media/Matrigel, 1:1) to the 29 gauge insulin syringe, cut the end of pipette tip evenly and transfer it with fine pipetting. We note that the amount of transferred cancer cells can vary greatly depending on the cutting part of tip and pipetting.
Anesthetize the mouse using 10μL/g of KX solution via intraperitoneal injection using 29 gauge insulin syringe.
About 10 minutes after anesthesia, make sure that the mouse is sufficiently anesthetized by applying pressure on the mouse toe with forceps.
Remove mouse hair under the ribs of the left flank using animal clipper.
Lay the mouse on Lt. decubitus position, pull the left leg and fix it with tape.
Spray 70% ethanol on the injection site and wipe with gauze.
Incise the muscle following by incision in the skin with a scissor.
Take out the pancreas with tissue forceps little by little from abdominal cavity of mouse and place on the gauze soaked in PBS.
Gently hold the pancreas with a tissue forceps and inject the cancer cell solution(Media/Matrigel, 1:1) with a syringe. A solution-filled region should be placed within the pancreatic parenchyma.
Slowly remove the syringe after injection.
After observing for 3 minutes to check hemorrhage or leakage, carefully re-place the pancreas into the abdominal cavity of mouse using a tissue forceps without touching.
Sequentially close the abdominal muscle and the skin with 5-0 vicryl, and monitor the mouse until the anesthesia is awakened.

The biggest disadvantage of the orthotopic pancreatic cancer model compared to the subcutaneous model is that it takes a long time to make the model, and tumor generation varies greatly depending on the skill of the researcher. Therefore, to reduce the time to create orthotopic models, we recommended that two or more researchers divide procedure into three main parts and proceed with each. - The first part is to anesthetize the mouse and shave its fur of the left flank area. - Second part is to make incision over left flank area of mouse, take out the pancreas, injects cancer cells into pancreas, and re-place the pancreas into the abdominal cavity. - Third part is sequentially close the abdominal muscle and the skin. - The most important part that directly impact on tumor growth is the second part. So we suggest that only one researcher should process the second part alone and the other parts are divided.
In the case of two experienced researchers conduct an experiment, it takes about 5-7 minutes to make one orthotopic pancreatic cancer model.
